items.markdown:
Quantitative easing (QE) is `an unconventional monetary policy where a central bank creates new digital money to buy financial assets, such as government bonds and mortgage-backed securities, directly from commercial banks and private investors`.[](https://www.stlouisfed.org/on-the-economy/2017/november/quantitative-easing-how-used) [[1]](https://www.stlouisfed.org/on-the-economy/2017/november/quantitative-easing-how-used)[[2]](https://www.stonex.com/en-us/business/financial-glossary/quantitative-easing/)

Why Central Banks Use It

Central banks (like the U.S. Federal Reserve or the Bank of England) typically lower short-term interest rates to stimulate a sluggish economy. However, when interest rates are already near zero and cannot be lowered further, they turn to QE. The primary goals are to:[](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/quantitative-easing) [[1]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/quantitative-easing)[[2]](https://www.ebsco.com/research-starters/business-and-management/quantitative-easing)[[3]](https://www.stonex.com/en-us/business/financial-glossary/quantitative-easing/)[[4]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp)

- **Lower long-term interest rates:** By buying large volumes of bonds, the central bank artificially drives their prices up and their yields (interest rates) down.[](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/quantitative-easing) [[1]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/quantitative-easing)[[2]](https://www.chicagobooth.edu/review/how-quantitative-easing-actually-works)
- **Inject liquidity:** By purchasing these assets, the central bank replaces them with cash in the banking system, encouraging banks to lend more freely to businesses and consumers.[](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp) [[1]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=llslyXPu6wI&t=3)
- **Boost asset prices:** The influx of cash pushes investors toward riskier assets (like stocks or corporate bonds), which can lift financial markets and support wealth creation.[](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp) [[1]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp)[[2]](https://www.stonex.com/en-us/business/financial-glossary/quantitative-easing/)

How It Works (Step-by-Step)

1. **Creation of Reserves:** The central bank digitally creates new reserves (money).
2. **Asset Purchases:** It uses these reserves to buy securities (like Treasuries) from major financial institutions.
3. **Multiplier Effect:** The banks now have excess cash, making them more willing to issue loans to the broader economy.[](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/quantitative-easing) [[1]](https://www.bankofengland.co.uk/monetary-policy/quantitative-easing)[[2]](https://www.stonex.com/en-us/business/financial-glossary/quantitative-easing/)[[3]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp)[[4]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=llslyXPu6wI&t=3)

Potential Risks While QE can stabilize markets during crises, it carries notable risks. If the money supply grows too fast and the economy cannot keep up, it can trigger high inflation or even devalue the national currency.[](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp) [[1]](https://www.investopedia.com/terms/q/quantitative-easing.asp)[[2]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O7g52EwkSGw&t=7)[[3]](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=llslyXPu6wI&t=3)How It Ends: Quantitative Tightening (QT)When the economy recovers and inflation becomes a concern, the central bank begins to reverse the process. This is known as **quantitative tightening (QT)** . During QT, the central bank stops replacing the bonds that mature or actively sells them back to investors, which removes money from the economy and raises long-term interest rates.[](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantitative_easing) [[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantitative_easing)[[2]](https://www.bankofcanada.ca/2025/02/understanding-quantitative-easing/)[[3]](https://www.investopedia.com/quantitative-tightening-6361478)To explore current and historical QE/QT actions, you can track the [Federal Reserve Balance Sheet](https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/bst_recenttrends.htm).[[1]](https://www.investopedia.com/quantitative-tightening-6361478)

items.title: Who benefits from quantitative easing?

expanded_element.title: What is Quantitative Easing (QE): benefits and risks | StoneX US
expanded_element.description: When quantitative easing is deployed to stimulate the economy, it can benefit lower-income households through job growth. The financial sector can also benefit from lower financing costs. While quantitative easing provides liquidity to financial markets, it can also lead to inefficiencies, such as asset bubbles.

items.title: What people are saying

Quantitative easing is counterfeiting with a graduate degree. 

Strip away the acronym and you find something a Roman emperor would recognize instantly. The Federal Reserve creates dollars out of nothing, buys government bonds and mortgage-backed securities from the primary dealers, and credits their reserve accounts with money that did not exist a keystroke earlier. That is the whole trick. Ben Bernanke launched the first round in November 2008, buying $600 billion in assets. By 2014 the Fed's balance sheet had swollen past $4.5 trillion. During the 2020 panic it blew past $8.9 trillion. Nobody mined anything. Nobody saved anything. They printed. 

Call it what it is: monetary debasement. The Fed prefers "asset purchase programs" because "printing money to fund the state" tests poorly with focus groups. 

You were told this was emergency medicine. Temporary. Bernanke sat before Congress and promised the balance sheet would normalize. It never did. Every attempt to shrink it, the 2018

$MACRO
QE vs QT 
Over half an year ago, we discussed the QE vs QT difference. 

What Quantitative Easing is, What Quantitative Tightening is. 

Since the last time we talked about this chart, the FED has added over $200B into the eceonomy, with NO CHANGE in interest rates since then. 

The last time interest rates were dropped signaling a potential QE was in Dec 2025. 

Now we have a different Fed Chair Warsh. A different vision, and that vision suggest NO CHANGE in interest rates for then next couple of months and potentially a HIKE in September. 

So, QT will last for more than half a decade this time. 

#CRYPTO  #TRADING  #MACRO  #QT  #QE  #FED  #WARSH  #MARKETUPDATE

The Fed says it has no intention of cutting rates. That's because the Fed is already engaged in stealth "quantitative easing" with its renewed balance sheet expansion – increasing at a 10% annual rate over the past four weeks!

Quantitative easing can boost output and inflation in a deep liquidity trap at lower cost than conventional fiscal policy, making it vital when fiscal space is limited, from Tobias Adrian, Christopher Erceg, Marcin Kolasa, Jesper Lindé, and Pawel Zabczyk https://lnk
Is Quantitative Easing QE Back

KEVIN WARSH SIGNALS A MAJOR FED SHIFT 🔥 Says QE and the Fed’s massive balance sheet are fueling inflation. Argues the Fed should exit markets outside of crises. This could reshape rates, liquidity, and risk assets.
Did Quantitative Easing Politicize the Fed? - ROI Podcast

Quantitative Easing (QE), whereby the Federal Reserve buys US Treasuries to finance the U.S. government’s deficit spending, is essentially printing money without restraint. 

By contrast, a revaluation of the gold owned by the US Treasury (8,133 metric tons) would provide the US government with a finite liquidity boost; approximately $1 trillion at a gold price of $4,000 per ounce. Issuing gold bonds would theoretically impose discipline on U.S. government spending, because the gold would eventually have to be repaid. 

Consequently, QE and unchecked deficit spending erode foreign sovereigns’ confidence in US Treasury bonds, while a US Treasury gold revaluation and the issuance of gold bonds would strengthen that confidence. 

It doesn’t matter what regular people think of this. They are not foreign sovereign buyers of U.S. Treasury bonds.

🚨 The Federal Reserve has injected $172 BILLION into the economy in just 4 months. They may not call it QE… but “Not QE, QE” looks like it has officially begun. 🚀 More liquidity. More risk appetite. More fuel for markets.
